narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a Black woman applying to law school, you've shown determination in pursuing this path despite the challenges many face, and I want to help you understand the automated decision that led to your application's rejection in a clear way. The admission system uses a predictive model trained on data from thousands of students like you, looking at factors such as early and later school grades, LSAT scores, undergraduate GPA, family income, and whether you'd study full-time to forecast your chances of passing the bar exam on the first try. For you specifically, with your Year 1 school decile of 2 (well below the typical 5.7 average), Year 3 decile of 1 (compared to the usual 5.5), LSAT score of 28 (versus the average 37), undergraduate GPA of 2.7 (below the 3.2 norm), middle family income bracket, and full-time study plans, the model calculated a 95.8% probability that you would fail the bar exam initially. This high predicted risk of failure is why your application was denied—the school aims to admit those with stronger projected odds of bar success to maintain their standards. The most influential factor pushing toward this failure prediction was your low Year 3 school decile ranking, which stood out as a major red flag compared to most students. Your Year 1 decile, also in the bottom ranks, reinforced this concern significantly, signaling early academic struggles that persisted. Your LSAT score further tipped the scales toward failure, as it was notably lower than what the model sees in successful bar takers. Less impactful but still contributing were your middle family income and undergraduate GPA, both below typical levels for those who pass. The one factor slightly favoring passage was your plan to study full-time, which aligns with the vast majority (over 90%) of students and is generally positive. Overall, your profile compares to typical admitted students by having weaker academic markers across the board, especially in school deciles and LSAT, which outweighed the neutral aspects like family background. While this is disappointing, especially given the barriers Black women often navigate in these processes, it's not the end—consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score, strengthening your later academic deciles through additional coursework, or targeting schools with holistic reviews that value your unique story. You have the resilience to improve these key areas and reapply stronger; many do. Keep pushing forward.
